question: On what mountain was Moses when the people made the golden calf?

memoryAid.text: Sinai and Horeb are one mountain with two names: the same peak where the burning bush blazed (Horeb) is where the Law now thunders (Sinai). Mark the dreadful symmetry: on the very height where Moses receives the finger-written Law, the people below carve their calf, so the tablets descend and shatter against the idol. The mount of revelation becomes the mount of betrayal.

question: Who was on the mountain with Moses, having gone up partway?

memoryAid.text: Joshua is Yeshua, the Hebrew name we render 'Jesus.' The one man who climbs the mountain alongside the lawgiver literally bears the Savior's name, and it is he, not Moses, who will finally carry Israel across into the inheritance: the Law brings us to the border, but Jesus carries us over.

question: What did the people ask Aaron to do?
answer: Make gods to go before them
options: Make gods to go before them
Build an altar to the Lord

memoryAid.text: Notice the plural: 'make us gods to go before us.' This is not atheism but a reversion to the gods of Egypt and the nations, the very elohim the Lord had just judged in the plagues. Israel is trying to re-enlist the defeated powers as their guides, craving a god they can carry and command rather than the unseen One who leads in cloud and fire.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: What material did the people use to make the golden calf?

memoryAid.text: Picture them tearing the gold rings from their own ears and their children's ears, the Egyptian plunder God gave them at the Exodus, gold meant to furnish His tabernacle, now ripped off and poured into the fire. The very ears made to hear 'Hear, O Israel' are stripped to forge an idol that cannot hear.

question: Aaron opposed the people and refused to make the calf.

memoryAid.text: Aaron AIDED, he did not avoid. He gathered the gold, shaped the calf with a tool, and built an altar before it with his own hand. The very man God was about to ordain high priest is the one who cast the idol. Remember: the priest fell first, and the people fell through him.

question: Complete Aaron's excuse for the golden calf: "I cast it into the fire, and out came this ____."

memoryAid.text: Aaron shrugs and claims the calf simply walked out of the furnace on its own: 'I threw in the gold and out came this cow,' as if the fire gave birth to a god. It is the oldest dodge in Scripture since Adam blamed Eve: sin never owns the hand that shaped it, it always blames the furnace.

question: Which group stood with Moses when he called, 'Who is on the Lord's side'?

memoryAid.text: LEVI for the Lord. When Moses cried 'Who is on the Lord's side?' the sons of Levi ran to him and drew the sword that day for God above kin. That zeal consecrated them: the tribe that took up the blade for the Lord becomes the tribe that stands forever at the altar. Levi = loyal = Levites.

question: About how many men died after the golden calf incident?

memoryAid.text: Three thousand fall by the sword over the calf when the Law comes down; three thousand are baptized at Pentecost when the Spirit comes down. The letter of the Law, struck against idolatry, kills three thousand in a day; the Spirit poured out adds three thousand to life. Scripture keeps the same number so you cannot miss the reversal.

question: Where was the tent of meeting (tabernacle of testimony) placed?
options: In the center of the camp
At the entrance to the camp

memoryAid.text: After the calf, the tent of meeting is pitched OUTSIDE the camp: to seek the Lord one had to walk out past the boundary, away from the defiled multitude, because His holy presence in the midst of a stiff-necked people would consume them. So our Lord too 'suffered outside the gate,' and we are bidden to go out to Him, bearing His reproach. The distance is the measure of the breach.

question: Everyone entered the tent of meeting together with Moses.

memoryAid.text: Only Moses goes in; the people watch and worship from their tent doors, far off. One mediator stands between the holy God and the multitude, the people drawing near through him alone. This is the office Christ fills perfectly, the single Man who enters the presence on our behalf and passes within the veil.

question: Which young man remained in the tent of meeting?

memoryAid.text: While Moses comes and goes, young Joshua, whose name is Yeshua, simply will not leave the tent. Picture the one who bears the Savior's name lingering inside the place of the Presence and refusing to depart: where God tabernacles, there 'Jesus' remains.

question: What did Moses request of God, saying 'Show me Your...'?

memoryAid.text: 'Show me Your glory' is the soul's deepest hunger, and St. Gregory of Nyssa makes it the heart of the whole spiritual life: the more of God you see, the more you long to see, forever. There is no ceiling on this ascent, because His essence is inexhaustible, and to find Him is only to long to seek Him further.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: Moses was permitted to see God's face directly.

memoryAid.text: 'No one shall see My face and live.' Moses, the friend of God, is shown not the unapproachable essence but the 'back parts,' the energies that stream toward us. St. Gregory reads this as the soul's entry into the divine darkness, where God is known not by comprehending Him but by following ever after Him.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses

question: What was Moses permitted to see as the Lord passed by?
answer: God's back parts / glory passing by
options: God's back parts / glory passing by
God's face shining like the sun

memoryAid.text: Picture Moses wedged into a cleft of rock, shielded by God's own hand, allowed to glimpse only God's back as He passes. The Fathers say that sheltering rock is Christ, for 'the Rock was Christ,' and to see only His 'back' means we know God by following where He has already gone, never by getting ahead to see His face.

question: How many new stone tablets did Moses cut for the renewed covenant?

memoryAid.text: TWO tablets, twice given. The first pair, written by God's own finger, Moses shattered at the sight of the calf; the second pair he quarried himself and carried up. Two tablets for the two great loves, of God and of neighbor, and the broken Law remade is itself a sign of mercy after the fall.

question: How many days and nights was Moses on the mountain renewing the covenant?

memoryAid.text: FORTY is the number of testing before a threshold: forty days of flood-rain, forty years in the wilderness, forty days of Christ's fast against the tempter. Moses' forty days on the mount are the same ordeal that always stands just before a covenant. Wherever Scripture writes forty, a threshold is being crossed.

question: Moses ate bread and drank water during the forty days on the mountain.

memoryAid.text: NO bread, NO water, forty days entire. Moses was sustained by the Presence alone, a living sign that 'man does not live by bread alone but by every word that proceeds from the mouth of God.' Remember the double 'no': what he lacked below, he received above.

question: What happened to Moses' face after he spoke with God?

memoryAid.text: Moses' face shone with borrowed glory that was already fading and had to be veiled. St. Paul sets it against our greater portion: the splendor of the old was passing like a setting sun, but we all, with unveiled face, behold the glory of the Lord and are changed from glory to glory, a radiance that does not fade but rises.

question: Why did Moses put a veil over his face?
answer: Because the people feared to approach him
options: Because the people feared to approach him
To hide his identity from enemies
To protect his eyes from the sun
Because God commanded him to hide the tablets

memoryAid.text: The people feared the glory, so Moses veiled his face, partly so they could not watch it fading. St. Paul reads that very veil as the obscurity still lying over the old covenant when it is read apart from Christ; but turn to the Lord, and the veil drops away, and the shining Moses is seen at last as a figure of Him who shone unveiled on Tabor.

question: Of what material was the altar of burnt offering made?

memoryAid.text: Bronze is the metal of judgment-by-fire all through the tabernacle: the bronze altar, the bronze serpent, the bronze laver. Incorruptible acacia wood sheathed in that fire-tried metal, the altar where the offering is consumed, already preaches the Cross, which judgment touched but corruption could not, for the incorruptible flesh of the Lord saw no decay.
memoryAid.source: Fr. Stephen De Young, The Whole Counsel of God (Ancient Faith, 2021)

question: The bronze laver was made from the mirrors of which women?
answer: The women who served/fasted at the tabernacle entrance
acceptableAnswers: the women who served at the tabernacle entrance
women at the door of the tabernacle

memoryAid.text: The serving women hand over the very mirrors they once used to admire themselves, and those vanity-glasses are melted down into the basin where the priests wash before approaching God. Surrendered self-regard becomes the instrument of purification: the looking-glass turned into a cleansing-glass.

question: What was the length of the tabernacle court?

memoryAid.text: A hundred cubits long by fifty wide, a clean 2-to-1 rectangle, twice as long as it is broad. Picture the long sacred hall exactly double its breadth; the hundred-cubit length is the larger number, the fifty its exact half. Hundred long, fifty wide.

question: What was the width of the tabernacle court?

memoryAid.text: Fifty wide to a hundred long: the width is exactly half the length. Hold the 2-to-1 ratio and both numbers fall out at once, so if you remember 'half as wide as it is long,' fifty drops out of a hundred automatically.

question: The tabernacle used silver for every single furnishing.

memoryAid.text: THREE metals, never one: Gold, Silver, Bronze (G-S-B, inner to outer). Gold for the holiest things nearest God, silver for the bases that bear the structure, bronze for the outer court. The glory literally diminishes as you move outward from the Presence, the metallurgy mapping the gradient of holiness.

question: How much gold was collected and used for the holy vessels?

memoryAid.text: Gold = 29 talents, 720 shekels. It is the smallest haul by weight yet clothes the things nearest God's presence: in the tabernacle, preciousness runs opposite to quantity. The closer to the Holy of Holies, the rarer and more glorious the metal. 29 talents, 720 shekels of the least-but-best.

question: How much silver was collected for the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: Silver = 100 talents, 1775 shekels. The hundred full talents are the redemption-money: every man over twenty paid a half-shekel ransom for his life in the census, and 603,550 half-shekels comes to exactly 100 talents. The price of a redeemed people becomes the bases the whole tabernacle stands on.

question: How much brass/bronze was collected for the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: Bronze = 70 talents, 1500 shekels, for the bases, pins, and altar of the outer court. Seventy, like the seventy elders and the seventy nations, is the number that faces the wider world, and bronze is fittingly the metal of the courtyard where the nations draw near.

question: The blue, purple, and scarlet materials were used to make what?
options: The veil of the tent only

memoryAid.text: Blue, purple, scarlet clothe AARON: heaven's blue, royal purple, sacrificial scarlet, the very colors of the veil now woven into the high priest's robes. He does not merely serve before the holy place; he wears it, a walking sanctuary, a living boundary between God and Israel.

question: How many men aged twenty and upward passed the survey/census?
acceptableAnswers: six hundred three thousand five hundred fifty

memoryAid.text: Anchor it to a number you already know: 603,550 = the 600,000 who marched out of Egypt (Ex 12:37) PLUS 3,550. 'Six hundred thousand, plus three-five-fifty.' The Exodus host, now counted to the man at Sinai.

question: Which of the following was NOT among the metals collected for the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: Gold, Silver, Bronze, but never IRON. Iron is the metal of swords and chariots, of war and the forge of violence; recall that no iron tool was to be lifted against the stones of the altar. The house of the God who makes peace bears no weapon-metal.

question: On what date was the tabernacle erected?
answer: The first day of the first month of the second year
options: The first day of the first month of the second year
The tenth day of the seventh month
The fifteenth day of the first month
The first day of the third year

memoryAid.text: First DAY, first MONTH, second YEAR. The tabernacle rose on the first of the first, almost exactly one year after the Passover night, a new-year's day on which God comes to dwell. Hold the triple 'first,' then remember it is the SECOND year.

question: About how long had the Israelites been out of Egypt when the tabernacle was erected?

memoryAid.text: One year out of Egypt, and only now does God come to dwell. Picture a whole year of law, wandering, and even the golden calf and its repair passing before a place could be made for the Presence: salvation in the spring, indwelling a full year later. The dwelling of God is the fruit of long preparation.

question: What was the FIRST step in setting up the tabernacle?

memoryAid.text: The order is fixed: Erect, Furnish, Wash, Anoint. First the house is raised, then filled with its vessels, then the priests are cleansed, then anointed. Structure before service, the dwelling before the indwelling. Begin with the standing of the tent.

question: What was the LAST of these steps in consecrating the tabernacle and priesthood?

memoryAid.text: ANOINTING comes last: Erect, Furnish, Wash, then ANOINT. The oil that seals the priests for service is the final act, the chrism that crowns all the preparation and sets them apart. Last step = anointing.

question: What filled the tabernacle when it was completed?

memoryAid.text: The glory of the Lord filling the tabernacle is the goal the whole book of Exodus has driven toward: God comes to dwell with His people. St. John deliberately echoes it, for 'the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we beheld His glory.' Exodus 40 is the first draft of the Incarnation, the cloud-filled tent foreshadowing the flesh that would hold the fullness of God.

question: Moses was able to enter the tabernacle after God's glory filled it.

memoryAid.text: Here is the shock: Moses, who entered the cloud on Sinai for forty days, now cannot even step into the tent, for the glory has filled it utterly. The friend of God is barred not by sin but by sheer excess of Presence, for no mere man can stand within unmediated glory until a greater Mediator opens the way within.

question: What was the function of the cloud over the tabernacle?
options: It provided rain for the camp
It marked the place of sacrifice
It hid the camp from enemies

memoryAid.text: Cloud UP, move out; cloud DOWN, stay in town. The same pillar of cloud and fire from the Exodus now rests on the tabernacle and sets the pace of every march. Israel does not navigate, it follows God's presence: the cloud is the compass.

question: Israel traveled even when the cloud remained resting over the tabernacle.

memoryAid.text: They moved ONLY when the cloud lifted; while it rested, Israel did not stir a single step. To march without the cloud would be to journey without God, and a pilgrim people that outruns the Presence is simply lost in the wilderness alone. They wait on the cloud, never the cloud on them.

question: When the cloud ascended from the tabernacle, what did Israel do?
answer: They set out on their journey
options: They set out on their journey

memoryAid.text: Cloud rises, camp rises: the ascending cloud is itself the marching order. When the Presence lifts, Israel lifts its tents and follows; resting cloud says 'stay put.' Israel reads its whole itinerary off the going and resting of God's glory above the tent.

question: How did the Lord speak with Moses at the tent of meeting?
answer: Face to face, as a man speaks to his friend
options: Face to face, as a man speaks to his friend
Only from within the pillar of cloud
Through dreams and visions in the night
Only by the hand of Aaron

memoryAid.text: Set this beside 33:20 (“no one can see My face and live”) and it sounds like a flat contradiction — but “face to face” names the intimacy of direct, friend-to-friend converse, not a sighting of God’s essence. Moses speaks WITH God as a friend, yet may still glimpse only His “back parts,” His glory; the unapproachable “face” of the divine essence no creature sees and lives.
memoryAid.source: St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses (cf. Exodus 33:11, 20)

question: After the golden calf, what reason did the Lord give for refusing to go up in the midst of Israel?
answer: Because they were a stiff-necked people
options: Because they were a stiff-necked people
Because the tabernacle was not yet built
Because Moses had asked Him to wait outside
Because the camp had become ritually unclean

memoryAid.text: Because of the calf, the Lord will not go “in the midst” of a stiff-necked people lest He consume them, so the tent stands outside the camp — fellowship ruptured. The estrangement is temporary: the tabernacle He commands will rise in the heart of the camp, God dwelling among His people once more — the road that ends in the Word who “tabernacled among us.”
